entry.title: Re: Q&A section to have Public Cloud or Private Cloud option to choose
entry.content:
Thank you for clarifying the current rules of engagement and the role of SAP Managed tags. I fully appreciate the intent;  ensuring that posts carry the right product version and environment context makes it much easier for contributors to provide accurate answers.
At the same time, given the high volume of questions in the forum, it is not practical to ask every poster individually about whether they are on Private Cloud or Public Cloud. This adds overhead for contributors who are already investing significant time in supporting the community.
To make the process more efficient and reduce ambiguity, my suggestion is that the system itself should enforce the selection of the environment (Private Cloud or Public Cloud) before a new post can be created. That way, contributors can focus on solving the issue rather than clarifying the context, and the community benefits from cleaner, more precise discussions.
I remain committed to helping members, but I believe this small improvement would save time for everyone and ensure that posts are consistently tagged with the right environment.

entry.title: Re: Serial Number is locked by User error in MIGO
entry.content:
This is not a functional error but a locking mechanism. If it happens frequently, check with your basis team whether database commit times are unusually long. In some releases, SAP has delivered notes to improve serialization performance and lock handling. Search for OSS notes related to “serial number locked by user” in refurbishment scenarios.

The Best Practice approach is to use the Refurbishment Order flow as intended. Transfer posting → order processing → goods receipt. Avoid running both postings back‑to‑back in parallel sessions. If automation is required, build a small delay or commit check into the custom program or workflow.

Our questions are:

  1. Are there any tools and/or test specification provided by SAP to test our implementation for SAP S/4Hana on premise?
  2. Is there a test specification including in and output parameters provided by SAP to test our implementation for SAP S/4Hana on premise?
  3. Is it possible to certify our CMIS API for SAP S/4Hana on premise installations?

Best regards,
Michael

entry.title: Re: PO created via BAPI_PO_CREATE1 cannot be used for GR...
entry.content:
In ME21N, the system automatically determines pricing conditions and sets key fields such as NET_PRICE, PRICE_UNIT, and the GR indicator. In BAPI_PO_CREATE1, if you don’t explicitly pass these fields (or the corresponding X structures), the PO item may be created without a net price or without the GR indicator set. A PO item without a valid net price/condition or without GR relevance will not appear in MIGO for goods receipt.

So to answer your questions

1) Can missing/incomplete pricing cause items to be non‑selectable in MIGO?
Yes. If the item has no net price or is not flagged GR‑relevant, MIGO will not show it.

2) What needs to be populated to finalize pricing like ME21N?
Either pass valid condition records in POCOND/POCONDHEADER, or explicitly set NET_PRICE and PRICE_UNIT in POITEM. ME21N does this automatically via condition technique; the BAPI requires you to supply it.

3) Are there any commonly missed mandatory fields

  • POITEM-GR_IND (Goods Receipt indicator)
  • POITEM-NET_PRICE and POITEM-PRICE_UNIT
  • POITEM-CHANGE_ID (to trigger condition update)
  • Condition type (e.g. PB00) in POCOND

entry.title: Accessibility - Data tables
entry.content:
After an accessibility audit on SAP SuccessFactors, here are some non-compliances that were noted regarding data tables, that we would appreciate to see evolve.

  • Data tables must have a title
  • Column headers must be linked to their cells using `scope=‘col’`
  • Column headers must not be empty

Example:

entry.title: Re: Two different company codes have two different plants...
entry.content:
Purchasing org responsibility is always validated against the plant → company code chain. Even if you assign the purchasing org directly to the plant, the system still checks that the plant belongs to a company code and that the purchasing org is valid for that company code context. If the purchasing org is not explicitly linked to the company code, the consistency check fails, and you see “Purchasing Organization … not responsible for plant …”.

Having said that, you need both assignments. The plant assignment alone is not sufficient, because the system still validates the purchasing org against the company code of the plant.

entry.title: Re: How to verify email delivery for invoices in S/4HANA...
entry.content:
Go to the Manage Billing Documents app (or the relevant output management app). Open the invoice in question. In the Output section, either:

  • Use Resend Output (if the original output request exists), or
  • Create a new output request for the same invoice.

The system will then send the invoice again, this time to the corrected email address.

entry.title: Reversing Steps in Outbound of SAP Logistics Management – Reversal of an LTL Outbound Process
entry.content:
Mistakes happen. Orders get cancelled at the last minute. A warehouse operative picks the wrong item. The item got broken during picking/packing and is not “shippable” anymore. In a traditional warehouse system, unwinding these situations often means a painful sequence of workarounds, manual stock corrections, and support tickets.

SAP Logistics Management takes a different approach: every major step in the outbound process can be reversed or cancelled in a controlled, system-consistent way. This post walks through each reversal point from order to departure — what triggers it, where you do it, and what the system does behind the scenes.

 

The Big Picture: A Chain of Reversals

The outbound process in SAP Logistics Management moves through four phases, and each phase has its own reversal actions:

  1. Outbound Delivery & Logistical Demand — Decline or cancel before warehouse work begins
  2. Transportation Planning — Cancel tendering or subcontracting
  3. Warehouse Activities — Undo picking, packing, staging, or goods issue
  4. Transportation Execution — Reverse carrier arrival, loading, or departure

 

The key principle: work backwards. Each step can only be reversed if the subsequent steps haven’t been completed yet — or if those subsequent steps are reversed first. So let’s go backwards and start with a scenario where the truck departure has already been set and goods issue has already been posted. Let’s reverse everything at the example of an LTL (less than truck load) outbound process:

 

Phase 1: Reversing Transportation Activities

The pickup document tracks the physical handover of freight to the carrier. All three milestones — arrival, loading, departure — can be reversed.

Reverse Carrier Departure

Done in Manage Pickups and Deliveries.

The departure reversal has no prerequisites — it can always be triggered. This handles situations like missing freight data or an incorrect check-out time that needs to be corrected before handover documentation is finalised.

 

Reverse Loading Completion

Done in Manage Pickups and Deliveries.

If freight was loaded incorrectly, loading completion can be reversed, provided the carrier hasn’t departed.

Important for LTL without transportation planning: Consignments are created automatically when loading is confirmed. Reversing loading completion cancels those consignments. When loading is confirmed again, new consignments are created fresh based on the assigned transportation requests.

 

Reverse Carrier Arrival

Done in Manage Pickups and Deliveries.

If a carrier was checked in with incorrect data (wrong time, wrong dock check-in data e.g., like an incorrect license plate number), you can reverse the arrival — as long as loading hasn’t been completed and the carrier hasn’t departed.

 

Phase 2: Reversing Outbound Warehouse Activities

This is where most of the day-to-day reversal scenarios occur — stock discrepancies, damaged goods, last-minute order changes.

Reverse Goods Issue

Done in Manage Outbound Warehouse Activities.

This is the most significant warehouse reversal — undoing the stock movement in S/4HANA. Common triggers include operational errors, last-minute cancellations, or incorrect staging.

The process is asynchronous: when you trigger the reversal, the outbound warehouse request immediately shows Goods Issue Reversal In Progress. The actual reversal happens in SAP S/4HANA:

  • Success: GI status resets to Not Started, lifecycle status returns to Open, and stock and handling units are recreated in SAP Logistics Management.
  • Failure: GI status remains Completed and lifecycle status switches to Completed — indicating the reversal needs to be investigated in S/4HANA.

 

Reverse Staging

Done in Manage Outbound Warehouse Activities.

Staging reversal covers situations where goods have been moved to the staging area but need to go back — for example, damaged items discovered during loading, or a last-minute process change.

The system behaviour differs by shipping mode:

  • LTL process: The reference to the outbound delivery is removed from the pickup document and consignment.
  • Direct LTL process: Packaging information is removed from the pickup document and consignment.

In both cases, the warehouse processing status of the transportation request is reset to Ready, allowing warehouse processing to resume.

Prerequisite: Goods issue must not have been posted and loading must not be marked as completed.

 

Reopen Handling Units and Unpack Items

Done in Packing Work Center.

A packed handling unit can be reopened when items inside are found to be damaged or an order is cancelled post-packing. Reopening moves the HU back to the Open HUs section, unpacked items become available for re-packing, and the packing status on the outbound warehouse request is reset to Open.

Note for parcel processes: If a label has already been printed (which auto-closes the HU), reopening is blocked. You’ll need to reverse staging first.

 

 

Release Stock From an Outbound Warehouse Request

Done in Manage Stock.

Once stock has already been picked (warehouse task completed), you can’t simply cancel the task. Instead, you release the stock from the outbound warehouse request. This effectively undoes the picking assignment without creating a return warehouse task — the stock stays physically where it was picked to, but is no longer tied to the outbound delivery.

Prerequisites: Stock must not be packed in a handling unit and staging must not be completed. If it is, you need to reopen the HU or reverse staging first.

 

Phase 3: Declining the Outbound Delivery or Cancelling the Logistical Demand

Cancel Logistical Demand

Done in Monitor Logistical Demands.

This is the more comprehensive cancellation. When you cancel a logistical demand, the system automatically cascades the cancellation to all follow-on documents: the outbound warehouse request is cancelled and the transportation request is deleted and an integration task is triggered to decline the corresponding outbound delivery in S/4HANA.

Prerequisites: Cancellation without restrictions is possible when processing hasn’t started. Once processing is underway, the following must all be true:

  • No active tendering
  • No active subcontracting
  • No physical warehouse activities started, i.e. no picking warehouse tasks are created or even confirmed
  • Carrier arrival not yet confirmed

If any of these conditions aren’t met, you need to reverse the relevant downstream steps first before you can cancel the logistical demand.

 

This is how the outbound warehouse request looks like:

 

And the outbound delivery in SAP S/4HANA:

 

Summary

The reversal chain in SAP Logistics Management is designed so that each step cascades cleanly — the system tells you what needs to be undone first if prerequisites aren’t met, guiding you through the sequence rather than leaving you to figure it out manually.

 

Want to learn more about SAP Logistics Management?

Explore the SAP Logistics Management Online Course 

Visit the SAP Logistics Management Product Page

Have a look at our Roadmap

Read to online help Reverse and Cancel Steps in the Outbound Process — SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Revaluation of Fixed Asset – Posting in a Separate Document
entry.content:
Currently, the revaluation of assets is posted in the AFAB document during the depreciation run, and the amounts are compressed based on the GL accounts.

Would it be possible to post the revaluation of fixed assets in a separate document and for each asset individually? Can this be customized? This is a legal requirement in Turkey for tax regulations.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Revaluation of Fixed Asset – Posting in a Separate Do...
entry.content:
Currently, your system posts revaluation amounts during the depreciation run (AFAB) in compressed format within the same document, grouping amounts by GL accounts rather than individual assets. This standard behavior consolidates revaluation postings to minimize document volume but reduces asset-level visibility in the GL postings [1],.

The revaluation amounts are calculated and stored at the asset level but posted to GL in summarized form, making it difficult to trace individual asset revaluations directly from the financial documents.

Legal Requirement Assessment

Turkey's tax regulations require individual asset revaluation postings for compliance purposes, necessitating separate documentation for each asset's revaluation amount. This requirement conflicts with SAP's standard compressed posting approach and requires customization to meet local regulatory standards.

The individual posting requirement ensures proper audit trails and supports detailed asset-level reporting required by Turkish tax authorities.

Technical Solution Options Option 1: Configuration Changes

Customization Path: Navigate to SPRO → Financial Accounting → Asset Accounting → Special Valuation → Revaluation of Fixed Assets → Revaluation for the Balance Sheet [1],

Document Splitting Configuration:

  • Configure separate document types for revaluation postings
  • Modify posting rules in transaction OAYR to enable individual asset posting
  • Set up account determination to use different GL accounts for individual revaluations [1],

Posting Logic Adjustments:

  • Enable "Post Revaluation" checkbox in depreciation posting rules
  • Configure revaluation areas to support individual postings
  • Set up separate revaluation offsetting accounts for each asset class [1],

Option 2: Custom Development

Enhancement Points: Utilize BAdI implementations like FAA_DOCLINES_COUNTRY or BADI_FIAA_DOCLINES to customize document line item creation during revaluation posting [2]

Custom Program Requirements:

  • Develop custom posting program to create individual documents per asset
  • Implement logic to bypass standard compression during revaluation posting
  • Create custom transaction type for Turkey-specific revaluation requirements

Although issued for other countries, still the SAP Notes are to be considered: 

SAP Note 3191438 - Romania,Serbia:Revaluation of Fixed Assets: Non-zero balance in case of transferred reserves, Mapping Additional Document Line Items to Fixed Asset Numbers, Incorrect Amounts in Company Code & Global Currencies

SAP Note 3212185 - Revaluation of Fixed Assets for Romania and Serbia: Implementation Leading SAP Note

SAP Note 3410289 - Revaluation of Fixed Assets for Romania and Serbia: Standard Implementation Delivered for C-CEE Add-On

et al.

entry.title: Re: Compatibility Pack: How to check it in your system going forward?
entry.content:
Dear Imran Hasware,
as explained in note 2269324, for SAP S/4HANA systems operated under a cloud subscription contract, the Compatibility Pack use rights for all items will expire Dec 31, 2033. Contract signature date is important here. If valid cloud subscription contracts are in place by end of May, the transition from on premise to our private cloud could be completed even later. For this period of time, you will be allowed to continue using compatibility packages in your on premise system.

This means, you can still use compatibility package functionalities  in your SAP S/4HANA 1909 system until completion of the transition to SAP Private Cloud in July.
Best regards,
Stefan

entry.title: Get text via SAP-Scripting from type GuiShell Subtype HTMLViewer
entry.content:
I automate SAP with VBA and SAP-Scripting-Interface and I actually need your help, please

When a business partner is opened, SAP displays warnings in a separate bar, e.g., "Please clarify address." I would like to extract this text, but it doesn't seem to be embedded in the element's structure like it would be in a popup. This bar isn't present when there are no warnings, and when there is a warning, it consists of two child elements.

wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[0]/shell --> The button to hide the entire bar
wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[1]/shell --> The area containing the text

Some properties of the element containing the text:
BrowserHandle: Nothing
Children.Count: 0
Handle: 788884
ID: wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[1]/shell
SubType: HTMLViewer
Text: SAP.HTMLControl.1
Type: GuiShell

What I have found so far in my research always leads to the solution of reading the InnerText of the document's body via the BrowserHandle, just as Google's AI does, for example

            Dim shellObj, browserObj, doc, text
         
            Set shellObj = session.FindById("wnd[0]/shellcont[0]/shellcont/shell/shellcont[1]/shell")
            Set browserObj = shellObj.BrowserHandle
            Set doc = browserObj.Document
            text = doc.body.innertext

That won't work for me because the BrowserHandle is set to Nothing.
For your information, since many posts I found mentioned problems with the BrowserHandle in SAP 770, I'm working with SAP 800, patch level 14.

Does anyone have any idea how I can access the text that is displayed in this element?

entry.title: Re: MM01 Real time check Classification
entry.content: You need to move the check into a material master save exit/BAdI that can read classification after it Is entered. BADI_MATERIAL_CHECK alone won’t work because classification isn’t yet committed at that point. So you can try with EXIT_SAPLMGMU_001 or BAdI_MATERIAL_MASTER to enforce the rule at save time. Also, in your code, try calling FM CLAF_CLASSIFICATION_OF_OBJECTS with the material number and class type (usually 001 for material). If no classification entries are found, issue an error (MESSAGE e...) to prevent save.

entry.title: Re: Calculating Manager input in the comment section for CDP
entry.content:
@William_Yu1I am not able to add a image here of the data model but I will do my best to explain the structure.

Job Information > Primary Goal Fields / Legal Entity / Primary Goal Fields / Form > Competency / Form Section - Section Comment, Section ID and Section Name selected.

Here is a further breakdown of the formula created to capture if the comment has been left for the section or no:

IF( [Performance Management#Form Section#Section ID] =2, IF(ISNULL([Performance Management#Form Section#Section Comment]), 0, 1), 0 )

Formula to capture if the comment has been left by Manager or Employee, as we don`t have separate field for manager comment in CDP.

IF( [SECTION_3] = 1, IF( ISNULL([Form Section#user_information_sac#Basic User Information#Section Comment Owner User ID]), 0, IF( [Form Section#user_information_sac#Basic User Information#Section Comment Owner User ID] = [User#Basic User Information#Employee ID], 0, 1 ) ), 0 )

Capturing Sum of Manager Comments: IF(ISNULL([M_C]), 0, [M_C]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_1]), 0, [M_C_1]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_2]), 0, [M_C_2]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_3]), 0, [M_C_3]).

The story is a table format at the moment and starts with Manager Name, Employee ID, Employee Name, Current Route Step Name, Current Role, Manager_Comment_My Current Role, Areas of Strength, Action Plan Goal, Manager_Comment_Area of Strength, etc.,

Do you have any best practice how to capture and calculate Manager Input in CPD?

entry.title: How to Fix Groovy Script Warnings and Upgrade to Script Version 2.x in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
What Changed in the March 15, 2026 Release

SAP introduced enhanced design guideline checks for Groovy scripts in integration flows.

These checks help developers ensure their scripts:

  • Avoid unsupported libraries

  • Avoid wildcard imports
  • Remove duplicate or unused imports
  • Use supported runtime classes
  • Remain compatible with Groovy runtime 4.0.29

The script editor now categorizes issues into:

  • Errors
  • Warnings
  • Information

A new Upgrade Readiness Check highlights script incompatibilities that could break in future runtime upgrades.

Unlike some design guideline checks, Groovy script compatibility issues cannot be skipped, meaning they must be addressed.

What I Noticed After the Update

After the March 15 release update, I reviewed one of my integration flows and noticed that the Groovy script used in my Exception Subprocess was showing warnings in the script editor.

The script itself was still working correctly and running smoothly, and there were no runtime errors in the integration flow. However, the editor flagged some upgrade readiness warnings, mainly related to wildcard imports and compatibility with future runtime upgrades.

Once I upgraded the script to Groovy Script Version 2.x, the editor automatically resolved the issues and removed the warnings, making the script fully compliant with the latest SAP design guidelines.

Example Warning: Wildcard Import

One of the warnings highlighted was:

Usage of Wildcard (*) imports on Internal Classes/Packages is not supportedand may be removed in future.

The script contained the following import:

 
import groovy.json.*;

Wildcard imports like this are now discouraged because they can introduce hidden dependencies and compatibility issues.

Fixing the Wildcard Import Issue

The solution was simple: replace the wildcard import with a specific class import.

Old Import

 

import groovy.json.*;

Fixed Import

import groovy.json.JsonBuilder

After replacing the import, the script complied with the new design guidelines.

Fix Script Incompatibilities Using the Script Editor

SAP has made it easier to fix issues directly inside the Groovy script editor.

Step 1 — Open the Groovy Script

Navigate to your integration flow and open the local Groovy script step.

Step 2 — Check the Problems View

The script editor automatically detects issues and displays them in the Problems tab.

Two major categories are shown:

Upgrade Readiness Check

Checks for:

  • Unsupported libraries
  • Deprecated classes
  • Wildcard imports
  • Duplicate imports

General Script Check

Checks general scripting practices.

Step 3 — Apply Automatic Fix

For issues that support automatic fixes:

  1. Select the issue
  2. Click Fix
  3. Confirm the suggested changes

The editor automatically updates the code.

Step 4 — Save and Deploy

After resolving the issues:

  1. Save the script
  2. Return to the integration flow
  3. Simulate or deploy the flow

Upgrading Groovy Script from Version 1.x to 2.x

Another useful feature is the direct upgrade option for Groovy scripts.

Why Upgrade to Groovy Script 2.x?

Upgrading provides several benefits:

  • Compatibility with Groovy runtime 4.0.29
  • Automatic fixes for incompatible code
  • Access to modern Groovy language features
  • Better support for future runtime upgrades

How to Upgrade the Script

  1. Open the Groovy script in the editor
  2. Click Upgrade
  3. Confirm the upgrade

All integration flow steps using this script will automatically move to Groovy Script Version 2.x.

SAP also keeps a backup of the previous script version in the Scripts folder in case you want to revert.

Real Example: Exception Subprocess Script Upgrade

In my case, the Exception Subprocess script was originally written using Groovy Script 1.x.

Old Script (Groovy Script 1.x)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;import groovy.util.XmlSlurper;import java.util.HashMap;import groovy.json.*;import org.apache.camel.Exchange;import org.apache.camel.builder.SimpleBuilder;import groovy.xml.*import groovy.util.*;

This script included:

  • Wildcard imports
  • Older SAP integration libraries

Updated Script (Groovy Script 2.x)

After clicking Upgrade, the script was automatically updated by the editor.

 
// This is Groovy Script Version 2.x, running with Groovy runtime 4.import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.Message;import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.language.SimpleBuilder;import groovy.json.JsonBuilder

Key improvements:

  • Uses the new SAP script API
  • Removes wildcard imports
  • Compatible with Groovy runtime 4

The functionality of the script remained the same, but the code is now future-ready and compliant with SAP standards.

Recommendation

After upgrading your scripts:

  • Always simulate the integration flow
  • Verify the message processing logs
  • Ensure the output matches expected behavior

Even though SAP provides automatic fixes, validation before production deployment is recommended.

Conclusion

The March 15, 2026 SAP Integration Suite update introduced helpful improvements for maintaining clean and upgrade-ready integration scripts.

Using the Upgrade Readiness Check and the Groovy Script 2.x upgrade option, developers can easily fix incompatibilities and align their scripts with SAP best practices.

In my case, upgrading the script and fixing the wildcard import warning resolved the issues highlighted by the editor and ensured my Exception Subprocess script is ready for future SAP runtime upgrades.

entry.title: RAP Unmanaged Draft: Early Numbering and Associations for Unique IDs
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

In RAP, Implementing numbering mechanisms during these operations is essential to ensure unique identifiers for newly created entities.
This blog is exposure the best for numbering during Creation and Creation by Association, in Unmanaged Scenario with Draft. How to handle number creation with Draft Scenario's.

Some Information about Numbering
Basically, Numbering is the concept to process assigning unique values to the key fields of business entities during Create and Create By Association Operations.

Unmanaged Early Numbering:

  • Developers need to implement custom code logic to create and assign keys values during the creation and create by association operation. 
  • %is_draft – It is a component of the transactional key (%tky) in RAP. 
    • It tells the framework whether to operate on the draft table or the active persistent table for a given operation. 
    • %is_draft = mk-on reads from draft table 
    • %is_draft = mk-off reads from active table 

Problem Statement

A sales clerk begins creating a Sales Order in Fiori. The order header is saved as a draft, but items need to be added immediately under that draft. To ensure consistency, the system must generate a unique Sales Order ID right away and assign incremental item numbers without waiting for activation.

Importance:

  • Early numbering guarantees unique identification across drafts and active records.

  • Associations ensure header–item linkage is preserved.

  • Draft handling allows users to save incomplete transactions safely and activate later without losing data integrity.

Behavior Definition of Root Entity

early numbering → enables key generation during root creation.

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_i_vd_head unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZI_VD_HEAD alias Header
draft table zdft_vd_head
lock master
total etag CreatedOn
authorization master ( instance )
early numbering // Early numbering declaration in root entity
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  association _item { create; }
  field ( readonly : update ) CustNo;

  draft action Edit;
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Resume;
  draft action Discard;
  draft determine action Prepare;
}
mapping for zvd_t_hsale {
  CustNo     = cust_no;
  SalesOrder = sales_order;
  CreatedOn  = created_on;
  EntryTime  = entry_time;
  Name       = name;
  DocCat     = doc_cat;
}


Behavior Definition of Item Entity

early numbering → enables key generation during item creation.

define behavior for ZI_VD_ITEM alias Item
draft table zdft_vd_item
lock dependent by _head
authorization dependent by _head
early numbering // Early numbering declaration in item entity
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) CustNo;
  association _head;

  mapping for zvd_t_isale {
    CustNo     = cust_no;
    SalesOrder = sales_order;
    DocItem    = doc_item;
    MatNumber  = mat_number;
    MatGroup   = mat_group;
    CreatedOn  = created_on;
  }
}

Behavior Pool Implementation

This method ensures that every new entity gets a unique identifier (CustNo) during creation if it wasn’t already provided.

UUID Generation:

  • cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ) generates a globally unique key.

  • This avoids collisions and guarantees uniqueness across drafts and active records.

Mapped Root:

  • %cid → correlates client-side temporary IDs with backend entities.

  • %key → holds the generated or existing key.

  • %is_draft → ensures draft state is preserved.

  • CustNo → the actual business key stored in the entity.

METHOD earlynumbering_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity) WHERE CustNo IS INITIAL.
    DATA(ls_uuid) = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).

    APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = entity-%cid
                    %key      = VALUE #( CustNo = ls_uuid )
                    %is_draft = entity-%is_draft
                    CustNo    = ls_uuid ) TO mapped-header.
  ENDLOOP.

  LOOP AT entities INTO entity WHERE CustNo IS NOT INITIAL.
    APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = entity-%cid
                    %key      = entity-%key
                    %is_draft = entity-%is_draft ) TO mapped-header.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Mapped Item:

This method handles early numbering when creating items via association (CREATE-BY-ASSOCIATION).

  • First, it retrieves the maximum cust_no from the database table zvd_t_isale for the current entity.

  • Then, it checks existing items in the helper class buffer (gt_isale) to ensure numbering continuity.

  • For each new item (DocItem IS INITIAL), it increments the maximum by 10 and assigns the new number.

  • %cid → client-side correlation ID.

  • %key → generated key with CustNo.

  • %is_draft → preserves draft state.

  • CustNo → ensures linkage to the root entity.

METHOD earlynumbering_cba_item.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).
    SELECT MAX( cust_no ) FROM zvd_t_isale
      INTO (lv_max)
      WHERE cust_no = -CustNo.

    LOOP AT zcl_vd_helper=>get_instance( )->gt_isale INTO DATA(item)
      WHERE cust_no = entity-%key-CustNo.
      IF item-doc_item > lv_max.
        lv_max = item-doc_item.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    LOOP AT entity-%target INTO DATA(t_item) WHERE DocItem IS INITIAL.
      lv_max += 10.

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = t_item-%cid
                      %key      = VALUE #( CustNo = entity-%key-CustNo )
                      %is_draft = t_item-%is_draft
                      CustNo    = entity-%key-CustNo ) TO mapped-item.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

  • Item inherits lock and authorization from the header.

  • Early numbering ensures item keys are generated consistently.

  • Association links items back to the header BO.

Persistence Methods

  • Create Method
METHOD create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).
    DATA(header) = CORRESPONDING zvd_t_hsale( entity MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).

    header-created_on = sy-datum.
    header-entry_time = sy-datum.
    header-name       = sy-uname.

    APPEND header TO gt_hsale.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
  • Create-By-Association Method
METHOD cba.
  LOOP AT entities_cba INTO DATA(entity_cba).
    LOOP AT entity_cba-%target INTO DATA(target_item).
      DATA(item) = CORRESPONDING zvd_t_isale( target_item MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).

      item-cust_no   = entity_cba-%key-CustNo.
      item-created_on = sy-datum.

      APPEND item TO gt_isale.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Result

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

By combining Draft Handling, Associations, and Early Numbering, RAP unmanaged scenarios can support complex business objects like Sales Orders, Purchase Orders. This approach ensures flexibility, control, and consistency in transactional applications.

Thank You...

entry.title: EML Create Business Partner Problem
entry.content:
I'm trying to create a Business Partner using EML (Entity Manipulation Language) with the standard interface view i_businesspartnertp_3 In SAP S4Hana Public Cloud.

The implementation executes without any runtime errors, and both FAILED and FAILED_COMMIT structures are initial. However, the Business Partner is not created in the system.

CLASS zcl_businessobject DEFINITION
PUBLIC
FINAL
CREATE PUBLIC .

PUBLIC SECTION.

INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun .
PROTECTED SECTION.
PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.




CLASS zcl_businessobject IMPLEMENTATION.


METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.


DATA create_partner TYPE TABLE FOR CREATE i_businesspartnertp_3.
create_partner = VALUE #( (
%cid = 'bp1'
businesspartnercategory = '1'
%control-businesspartnercategory = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
lastname = 'Test_BP5'
%control-lastname = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
) ).
MODIFY ENTITIES OF i_businesspartnertp_3
ENTITY businesspartner
CREATE FROM create_partner
CREATE BY \_businesspartneraddress
FIELDS ( country cityname ) WITH VALUE #( (
%cid_ref = 'bp1'
%target = VALUE #(
(
%cid = 'bp1_1'
country = 'IN'
cityname = 'Bangalore'
)
)
) )
MAPPED DATA(mapped)
REPORTED DATA(reported)
FAILED DATA(failed).
COMMIT ENTITIES
RESPONSE OF i_businesspartnertp_3
FAILED DATA(failed_commit)
REPORTED DATA(reported_commit).
IF failed_commit IS NOT INITIAL.
out->write( |Error| ).
ELSE.
out->write( |Sucess.| ).
ENDIF.


ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.


Any guidance on why the entity is not created despite no reported errors would be appreciated.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: Intangible assets
entry.content:
Hello @fendua ,

If I understood your ques correctly, in SAP On-Premise, you cannot define intangible assets as “Items” or “Itemized Services” in the asset master.

✔ Intangible classification is done via Asset Class
✔ “Item vs Service” is controlled in Purchase Order (ME21N) using Item Category

Alternate approach:-

Option 1 – Direct Capitalization (MM → FI-AA)

1️⃣ Create Asset (AS01) or auto-create via PO
2️⃣ PO (ME21N):

Account Assignment = A

Item Category = Standard / D
3️⃣ GR / SES → MIRO
4️⃣ Asset capitalization

Option 2 – AuC + Settlement (Best Practice for Intangibles like Development)

1️⃣ Create AuC (Asset Class: AuC – Intangible)
2️⃣ Procure via:

Service PO (D) OR Material PO

Account Assignment = A (AuC) or WBS
3️⃣ Periodic settlement (KO88 / CJ88)
4️⃣ Final asset capitalization

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: CAMT.053 no longer interprets the data medium referen...

this is a common issue when switching from MT940 format to CAMT because you now are in charge for the mapping of the data in SAP. And this is absolutely not the only problem to expect.

I have cerated a free LinkedIn How-To Article that describes how to handle the migration from MT940 to CAMT.053 in a safe and controlled way:

Andreas Knopf

entry.title: Re: Best Practice for Exporting Large Data Volumes (>1 Mi...
entry.content:
Hello @AliSaad ,

For 1M+ records in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the recommended approach is to use OData APIs with pagination and delta logic, or integrate with SAP BTP tools (Datasphere / Data Intelligence).
UI-based exports and CDS query downloads are not suitable for large-scale extraction and will fail due to system limitations.


If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Migration using Business Transformation Center (BTC) HCM
entry.content: Hi Matthias, Thank you for your reply. I have two more questions. Perhaps you can answer them as well. 1) in Note 3011960 states that: In general, from a technical perspective the transition to SAP HCM for SAP S/4HANA will be seamless/non-disruptive. Does this also mean that the migration from ECC to S/4HANA using BTC does not correspond to a system change? If that is the case, then all registration, deregistration, income tax audit etc. for social security and tax authorities would be unnecessary. 2) Since we are still using the old general ledger accounting system in the ECC, the accounting data will be left behind in the ECC and will not be transferred to S/4HANA. It is also planned that booking areas that have been deactivated or are no longer used will no longer be created in S/4HANA. However, in HCM there are employees who are assigned to these company codes and will be taken along to S/4HANA, as selective data transfer is not possible. In these cases, a calculation into the past must be excluded. Could this cause problems during the migration? Does the migration process check whether the company codes and controlling areas exist in S/4HANA? Or does it only transfer data to the corresponding HCM tables or cluster etc.? Best regards Petra

entry.title: Running Hugging Face AI Models with SAPUI5 and CAPM
entry.content:
Hi everyone !

After spending several hours researching and experimenting, I finally found a simple way to answer a question that many SAP developers have started asking:

  “Is it possible to run AI models inside SAP applications using SAPUI5 and CAPM?”

The short answer is:

   Yes, absolutely!

In this blog, I will share how we can integrate Hugging Face AI models with SAPUI5 and CAPM, and how we can build a simple AI-powered application that can generate text responses and even images.

First Question: What is Hugging Face?

 If you are new to AI development, you might wonder:

What exactly is Hugging Face?

Think of Hugging Face as GitHub for AI models.

It is a platform where developers and researchers publish and share machine learning models.

Currently, Hugging Face provides access to hundreds of thousands of AI models that can perform tasks such as:

  • Text generation
  • Chatbots
  • Image generation
  • Speech recognition
  • Translation
  • Code generation

The best part is that you can use these models directly through APIs without training your own model.

Another great advantage is that Hugging Face provides GPU infrastructure, which means you can run advanced models without owning expensive hardware.

And yes — if you build your own model, you can also publish it on Hugging Face and make it available globally.

        Fig 1.1 Hugging Face Models sections

 

Now the Interesting Part: Can SAPUI5 Use AI?

When I started this experiment, my first question was:

Can SAPUI5 directly call an AI model?

The answer is yes, especially for text generation models.

To make this work, we only need three things:

-  A Hugging Face account
-  An HF API Token
-  A model endpoint

Once you log in to Hugging Face, you can generate an HF Token from your account settings.

Then you can choose any model depending on your use case. Hugging Face provides model benchmarks and descriptions, so you can easily decide which one is best for:

  • Education
  • Coding
  • Reasoning
  • Chat
  • Content generation

Connecting Hugging Face with SAPUI5

Now let's talk about the implementation.

In the SAPUI5 controller, we can send a request to the Hugging Face API using JavaScript fetch().

The UI simply takes a user prompt, sends it to the AI model, and displays the response.

Example flow:

User enters a prompt:

       "What is Hugging Face and SAPUI5?"

SAPUI5 sends the prompt to the Hugging Face model.

The model generates a response and returns it.

Then SAPUI5 displays the result directly on the UI.

This makes it very easy to build AI chat assistants inside SAP applications.

         Fig 1.2 Output- Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

Sample Code:

      Fig 1.3 sapui5 Controller.js - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

        Fig 1.3 sapui5 view.xml - Hugging Face Ai Chat

 

But What About Image Generation?

This is where things become interesting.

When I tried running image generation models directly in SAPUI5, I realized something important.

⚠️SAPUI5 works best with text-based AI APIs.

For image generation models, we need a backend service.

This is where CAPM (Cloud Application Programming Model) becomes very useful.

 

 

Using CAPM for Image Generation

To generate images, we can integrate fal.ai, which provides inference APIs for advanced image generation models.

Some examples include:

  • black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev
  • Tongyi-MAI / Z-Image-Turbo

These models can generate high-quality AI images from text prompts.

For example:

"Portrait of a futuristic astronaut with glowing blue eyes"

The system sends this prompt to the AI model, and the model generates an image.

 

How CAPM Handles This

Inside the CAPM Node.js service, we create a custom action such as:

     "generateImage"

This action receives the prompt from SAPUI5.

Then CAPM calls the fal.ai API with that prompt.

The AI model generates the image and returns a URL of the generated image, which we send back to the UI.

This makes CAPM act as a bridge between SAPUI5 and AI models.

 

Why Use fal.ai Instead of Hugging Face Directly?

You might ask:

       If we already use Hugging Face, why do we need fal.ai?

          Good question.

Some models listed on Hugging Face are actually hosted through different inference providers, including fal.ai.

For example:

black-forest-labs / FLUX.1-dev

This model is often executed through fal.ai inference APIs.

So fal.ai becomes an easy way to run those models with strong GPU performance.

Sample Code:

 

Fig 1.4 Output – CAPM - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.5 service.js - first half- Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.6 service.js - second half - Hugging face Image generation

 

Fig 1.7 Fal.ai API key Generation - Hugging face Image generation

 

Final Result

After connecting everything together, we get a simple AI application:

The application can:

- Generate AI text responses using SAPUI5
- Generate AI images using CAPM services
- Use Hugging Face models
- Run without training your own model

All of this can be done using free developer APIs, unless you need large-scale GPU workloads.

 

Conclusion

From this experiment, we can see that SAP applications can easily integrate with modern AI platforms.

The architecture becomes very simple:

                   SAPUI5
                        ⬇
           CAPM Service
                      ⬇
   Hugging Face / fal.ai AI Models

 

Using this approach, developers can build powerful applications such as:

  • AI chat assistants inside SAP
  • AI-powered knowledge systems
  • Image generation tools
  • Smart enterprise automation

And the best part is that you can even deploy your own AI model on Hugging Face and use it inside SAP applications.

 

I hope this small experiment helps other developers who are curious about AI + SAP integration.

Happy coding!
