entry.title: How SAP S4HANA On-Premise (IDOC-Outbound) to SAP Integration Suite - Inbound IDoc Interface
entry.content:
Introduction

This guide provides  the configuration required to enable outbound IDoc communication from SAP S/4HANA to SAP Integration Suite. IDocs serve as SAP’s standard data exchange mechanism, and this scenario focuses on transmitting an (e.g., ORDERS, INVOIC, DESADV, MATMAS) IDoc from an on-premise S/4HANA system to an integration flow (iFlow) deployed on SAP BTP.

The configuration involves defining an XML HTTP port in S/4HANA and associating it with an RFC destination that targets the SAP Integration Suite iFlow endpoint. Authentication is implemented using Basic Authentication with service instance credentials obtained from SAP BTP. Secure communication is ensured by establishing an SSL/TLS connection through the import of the SAP Integration Suite server certificate into the S/4HANA Trust Manager.

Key components covered in this guide:

  • SAP GUI connection and S/4HANA login
  • Logical System configuration (Transaction BD54)
  • Define and Assigning Logical System (Transaction SALE)
  • RFC Destination creation for SAP Integration Suite (Transaction SM59)
  • Integration Flow design(artifacts) and deployment in SAP Integration Suite
  • BTP Cockpit – Service Instance Credentials retrieval
  • TLS Connectivity Test and Certificate import into Trust Manager (Transaction STRUST)
  • Partner Profile configuration (Transaction WE20)
  • EDI Message Type and Output Type assignment (WE81 / WE82)
  • IDoc Port setup for XML HTTP (Transaction WE21)
  • IDoc test dispatch via Test Tool (Transaction WE19/WE02)
  • Monitoring and verifying IDoc delivery in SAP Integration Suite and SAP S/4HANA 

Step 1: Connecting to SAP S/4HANA via SAP GUI

Before any configuration can begin, you need to establish a connection to your SAP S/4HANA system using the SAP GUI Logon application. This ensures you are working within the correct system and client.

1.1 — SAP Logon Connections

Open the SAP GUI Logon client. Under the Connections tab, verify that the S4_HANA entry is listed with the correct System ID (SID: A4H) and application server (dsposrvr.ds.com) at Instance 22. This connection entry is your gateway into the S/4HANA on-premise system.

Fig 1.1 – SAP GUI Logon showing the S4_HANA connection entry

1.2 — System Entry Properties

Double-click the S4_HANA entry (or right-click and select Properties) to review the System Entry Properties. Confirm that the Connection Type is set to "Custom Application Server" and that the System ID, Instance Number, and Application Server host are all correctly populated. Click OK to proceed.

Fig 1.2 – System Entry Properties dialog showing connection parameters

1.3 — Logging into the System

Log in to the SAP S/4HANA system using your credentials. In the SAP login screen, enter the Client (100), User ID (e.g., DSPO04), Password, and set the Logon Language to EN. The information panel on the right confirms you are connecting to the S/4 HANA OP (On-Premise) environment.

Fig 1.3 – SAP login screen with client 100 and user credentials

Step 2: Configuring Logical Systems (Transaction BD54)

Logical Systems are used in SAP to uniquely identify a client in a system landscape for IDoc communication. Before you can set up a partner profile or port, the SAP Integration Suite artifacts endpoint must be defined as a Logical System.

2.1 — Opening the Logical Systems Overview

Navigate Transaction BD54 to access the Change View "Logical Systems" screen. You will see a list of already-configured logical systems. Notice that entries such as CPI (cloud platform integration) and CPICLNT100 (CONNECTION TO CPI SERVER) are already present from prior configuration. Click "New Entries" to add any additional logical systems as needed.

Fig 2.1 – Logical Systems overview showing CPI-related entries

2.2 — Adding New Logical System Entries

If you need to create new entries, add the logical system name and its description in the fields provided. In this setup, two relevant entries are present: CPI for the cloud platform integration endpoint and CPICLNT100 as the connection to the CPI server. After entering the data, click Save to persist the configuration.

Fig 2.2 – New Entries screen showing CPI and CPICLNT100 logical system definitions

Step 3: Assigning Logical System to Client (Transaction /nSALE)

After defining the Logical Systems in BD54, the next mandatory step is to assign the Logical System to the correct SAP client. This binding tells the SAP system which Logical System name represents the current client, and it is a prerequisite for IDoc routing and ALE (Application Link Enabling) configuration.

Transaction /nSALE opens the SAP Implementation Guide (IMG) scoped to the ALE configuration area — IDoc Interface / Application Link Enabling (ALE). This is the central customizing entry point for all IDoc-related settings including Logical System assignment.

3.1 — Display IMG: IDoc Interface / ALE Settings

Execute Transaction /nSALE to open the Display IMG screen. The structure tree shows the ALE customizing hierarchy under "IDoc Interface / Application Link Enabling (ALE)." Expand "Basic Settings" to reveal the available configuration activities, which include: IDoc Administration, Inbound SOAP for IDoc: Register Service, Perform Automatic Workflow Customizing, Activate event receiver linkage for IDoc inbound, and Process Code for Inbound IDoc. Under the "Logical Systems" folder, two key activities are available: "Define Logical System" (which links to BD54) and "Assign Logical System to Client." Click "Assign Logical System to Client" to proceed.

Fig 3.1 – /nSALE IMG showing the IDoc Interface / ALE structure — navigate to Logical Systems → Assign Logical System to Client

3.2 — Assigning the Logical System to the Client

The Change View "Clients": Overview screen displays all SAP clients configured in this system. Each row shows the Client number, Role, Currency (Crcy), Name, City, and last Changed On date.

Fig 3.2 – Change View "Clients" showing all configured clients — assign the Logical System to Client 100

Step 4: Creating the RFC Destination to SAP Integration Suite (Transaction SM59)

The RFC Destination is the technical connection object in SAP that defines how SAP S/4HANA reaches the SAP Integration Suite endpoint over HTTPS. This is a Type G (HTTP Connection to External Server) destination.

4.1 — RFC Connection Overview

Open Transaction SM59 to access the Configuration of RFC Connections screen. Expand the "HTTP Connections to External Server" folder (Type G) this is where you will create the new destination pointing to CPI. Click the Create icon to start a new destination.

Fig 4.1 – SM59 RFC Connections overview showing the HTTP Connections to External Server category

4.2 — Creating the Destination

In the "Create Destination" dialog, enter the destination name as "SAP to CPI" and select the Connection Type as "G – HTTP connection to external server." Click the green checkmark to confirm and proceed to the destination detail screen.

Fig 4.2 – Create Destination dialog with name and connection type selected

4.3 — Technical Settings

On the Technical Settings tab of the RFC Destination "SAP to CPI," configure the target system parameters. In the Description field, enter "S4hana_Idoc" to identify this destination's purpose. Under Technical Settings (Target System Settings), set the Host to your CPI runtime URL (e.g., upskilling-xxxxxxxx.it-cpi004-rt.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com) and the Port to 443. For the Path Prefix, enter /cxf/idoc_inbound — this is the endpoint address of the deployed IDOC_Inbound integration flow in SAP Integration Suite.

Fig 4.3 – RFC Destination Technical Settings showing SAP Integration Suite host, port 443, and path prefix /cxf/idoc_inbound

4.4 — Logon & Security — Basic Authentication

Switch to the "Logon & Security" tab. Under the Logon Procedure section, select "Basic Authentication" as the logon method. Enter the Client ID obtained from the SAP BTP service instance credentials as the User and save the corresponding Client Secret as the password. This authenticates S/4HANA against SAP Integration Suite when sending IDoc messages.

Fig 4.4 – Logon & Security tab showing Basic Authentication with the CPI service account credentials

4.5 — SSL Configuration

Still on the Logon & Security tab, scroll down to the Security Options section. Ensure that SSL is set to "Active" and the SSL Certificate is set to "ANONYM SSL Client (Anonymous)".This enables encrypted communication over HTTPS between S/4HANA and SAP Integration Suite. You will later import the SAP Integration Suite server certificate into STRUST to complete the trust chain.

Fig 4.5 – Security Options showing SSL Active with ANONYM SSL Client (Anonymous) certificate

Step 5: Building the IDOC_Inbound Integration Flow Artifact in SAP Integration Suite

On the SAP BTP Integration Suite side, you need to create an integration package and an Artifact Integration Flow (iFlow) that will receive the IDoc sent from S/4HANA over HTTP/SOAP.

5.1 — SAP Integration Suite — Design Area

Log in to SAP Integration Suite. From the left-hand menu, navigate to Design → Integrations and APIs. This screen lists all available integration packages. Click Create in the top-right corner to create a new package for the IDoc integration scenario.

Fig 5.1 – SAP Integration Suite Design view showing the Integrations and APIs package list

5.2 — Creating the Integration Package

Fill in the package details: set the Name to "TestEDI" and the Technical Name to "TestEDI." Optionally add a short description. Set the Version to 1.0 and the Vendor to SAP. Click Save in the top-right corner to create the package.

Fig 5.2 – New package creation form for TestEDI with name, version, and vendor details

5.3 — Locating the IDOC_Inbound Artifact

After saving, navigate inside the TestEDI package to the Artifacts tab. You will see a list of integration flows. Locate the "IDOC_Inbound" integration flow (shown with a Draft status). This is the iFlow that receives IDoc data from S/4HANA. Click on it to open the editor.

Fig 5.3 – TestEDI package Artifacts tab showing the IDOC_Inbound integration flow in Draft status

5.4 — Configuring the IDOC Sender Adapter

Open the IDOC_Inbound integration flow. The flow consists of a Sender channel connected to a Start event and an End event — a simple pass-through that receives the IDoc payload. Select the IDOC sender adapter (the connection between the Sender participant and the Start step). In the Connection tab at the bottom panel, set the Address to /idoc_inbound, the Authorization to "User Role," and the User Role to "ESBMessaging.send." Click Save and then Deploy to activate the iFlow.

Fig 5.4 – IDOC_Inbound iFlow with sender adapter configured at address /idoc_inbound

5.5 — Monitor: Verifying Deployed Status

Navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs. The Manage Integration Content section shows a total of (e.g.,55 artifacts, with 52 started and 3 in error). Confirm that the IDOC_Inbound flow is counted in the "Started" group — this confirms it is running and ready to receive messages.

Fig 5.5 – SAP Integration Suite Monitor overview showing 52 started integration artifacts

5.6 — Retrieving the iFlow Endpoint URL

In the Monitor → Manage Integration Content screen, select IDOC_Inbound from the list on the left. On the right panel, navigate to the Endpoints tab. The full endpoint URL is displayed — for example: https://upskilling-xxxxxxxx.it-cpi004-rt.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com/cxf/idoc_inbound. Copy this URL — it will be used as the Host and Path Prefix values in the SM59 RFC Destination configured earlier. The Status Details panel confirms: "The Integration Flow is deployed successfully."

Fig 5.6 – IDOC_Inbound deploy details showing the endpoint URL and successful deployment status

Step 6: Retrieving CPI Service Instance Credentials from BTP Cockpit

To authenticate SAP S/4HANA against SAP Integration Suite using Basic Authentication, you need the OAuth Client ID and Client Secret from the SAP BTP service instance. These are used as the username and password in the SM59 RFC destination.

6.1 — BTP Cockpit — Instances and Subscriptions

Log in to the SAP BTP Cockpit and navigate to your subaccount (e.g., Upskilling). Go to Services → Instances and Subscriptions, then click the Instances tab. You will see three instances listed. Select the integration_flow instance under the "SAP Process Integration" service with plan "integration-flow." Click "keys" to view the service keys.

Fig 6.1 – BTP Cockpit Instances view showing the integration_flow service instance

6.2 — Viewing the Service Key Credentials

In the Credentials dialog, select "integration-auth-basic" from the dropdown and click the Form tab. The dialog reveals the OAuth credentials: the clientid (e.g., sb-xxxxxxxx-50d1-494f-b9df-xxxxxxb38cda!b31831...) and the clientsecret. The URL shown is your SAP Integration Suite tenant URL. Copy these credentials — the clientid will be used as the User in SM59, and the clientsecret as the password.

Fig 6.2 – Service Key Credentials showing clientid and clientsecret for Basic Authentication

Step 7: TLS Connectivity Test and Certificate Import

For secure HTTPS communication between S/4HANA and SAP Integration Suite, the server certificate used by Integration Suite must be trusted by S/4HANA. This is done by running a TLS connectivity test from CPI's monitor, downloading the certificate chain, and importing it into S/4HANA's SSL client PSE via Transaction STRUST.

7.1 — Monitor: Running the TLS Test

In SAP Integration Suite, navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs. On the overview dashboard, scroll down to the Manage Security section and click Connectivity Tests. In the TLS tab, enter the CPI runtime host (e.g., upskilling-xxxxxxxx.it-cpi004-rt.cfapps.ap11.hana.ondemand.com) and Port 443. Check "Valid Server Certificate Required" and click Send. The response on the right will confirm that the host was successfully reached and shows the Server Certificate Chain. Click "Add to keystore" to save it, or "Download" to download the certificate files for manual import into S/4HANA.

Fig 7.1 – SAP Integration Suit Connectivity Test confirming successful TLS connection and certificate chain

7.2 — SAP S/4HANA Trust Manager (STRUST) - SSL Client PSE

In S/4HANA, open Transaction STRUST (Trust Manager). In the left-hand tree, expand "SSL client SSL Client (Anonymous)" — this is the PSE (Personal Security Environment) used for outbound HTTPS calls. The Certificate List displays the currently trusted certificates. You need to import the SAP Integration Suite certificates here.

Fig 7.2 – Trust Manager showing the SSL client SSL Client (Anonymous) PSE and existing certificate list

7.3 — Switching to Change Mode and Preparing Import

Click the Change/Edit button (pencil icon in the toolbar) to switch the Trust Manager to Change mode. In the lower section, select the dsposrvr_A4H_22 certificate entry and click the Import Certificate button (the import icon at the bottom). This opens the Import Certificate dialog.

Fig 7.3 – Trust Manager in Change mode, ready for certificate import

7.4 — Selecting the Certificate File

In the Import Certificate dialog, click the file browse button next to the File path field. A file selection dialog will open. Navigate to the Downloads → certificates folder where you save the certificate chain downloaded from SAP Integration Suite. You will see three certificate files: the SAP Integration Suite server certificate and two DigiCert root/intermediate CA certificates. Select the appropriate certificate file and confirm.

Fig 7.4 – Import Certificate dialog prompting for the certificate file path

Fig 7.4 – File browser showing the three downloaded CPI certificates in the certificates folder

7.5 — Verifying and Adding to Certificate List

After selecting the file, the certificate details are displayed: Subject, Issuer (DigiCert G5 TLS RSA4096 SHA384 2021 CA1), validity dates (08.09.2025 to 08.09.2026), RSA algorithm, and key strength of 4096 bits. Click "Add to Certificate List" to add the certificate to the trusted list in the PSE, then click Save to persist the change.

Fig 7.5 – Certificate details displayed before adding to the SSL certificate list

Step 8: Testing the RFC Destination Connection

With the SSL certificate imported and Basic Authentication configured, you can now test the RFC Destination from SAP S/4HANA to verify that the connection to CPI is functional.

8.1 — Running the Connection Test

Return to Transaction SM59 and open the "SAP to CPI" RFC Destination. Click the "Connection Test" button in the toolbar. A warning dialog appears advising that an HTTP connection test depends on the service being called — nonexistent services return HTTP 404, and error details will be visible in the HTTP response. Click the green checkmark to proceed.

Fig 8.1 – RFC Destination SAP to CPI with the Connection Test button highlighted

Fig 8.2 – Warning dialog explaining HTTP connection test behavior — click the checkmark to proceed

8.3 — Interpreting the Test Result

The Connection Test HTTP Destination SAP to CPI results screen will show an HTTP Response Status of 500 (Internal Server Error). This is expected behavior at this stage — it means the HTTPS connection from S/4HANA successfully reached the SAP Integration Suite endpoint (the SSL handshake and authentication worked), but the SAP Integration Suite iFlow returned an error because no actual IDoc was sent in this test call. The Response Header Fields tab confirms the server is SAP Integration Suite, and the Response Body shows a SOAP Fault with an MPL ID pointing to the SAP Integration Suite message monitoring URL for details.

Fig 8.3 – Connection test result showing HTTP 500 — confirming the connection reached SAP Integration Suite successfully

Fig 8.4 – Response Body showing the SOAP Fault from SAP Integration Suite with the MPL monitoring URL

Fig 8.5 – Response Header Fields confirming HTTP/1.0 500 Internal Server Error from the SAP server

Note: An HTTP 500 response during this connection test is expected and confirms that the network connectivity, SSL, and authentication layers are all working correctly. The 500 error originates from CPI processing the empty test call.

Step 9: Configuring Partner Profiles (Transaction WE20)

Partner Profiles define how S/4HANA exchanges IDoc messages with external partners. In this scenario, CPI is configured as a Logical System partner, and outbound parameters are set to dispatch ORDERS IDocs to the SAP_CPI port.

9.1 — Partner Profiles Overview

Open Transaction WE20 (Partner Profiles). In the left-hand Partner tree, expand Partner Type LS (Logical system) and locate the CPI partner. The right panel shows Partner No. CPI with type LS and description "cloud platform integration." The Post Processing: Valid Processors tab shows the user agent is (e.g.,DSPO04). Scroll down to the Outbound section — you will see ORDERS already listed as a configured outbound message type.

Fig 9.1 – WE20 Partner Profiles showing CPI partner type LS with the ORDERS outbound entry

9.2 — Adding Outbound Parameters

Click the "+" button in the Outbound section to add a new outbound parameter entry. The Partner Profiles: Outbound Parameters screen opens for Partner No. CPI. Set the Message Type to ORDERS. You will now configure the Outbound Options (Receiver Port) for this message type.

Fig 9.2 – Outbound Parameters screen for partner CPI with Message Type ORDERS

9.3 — Selecting the Receiver Port

In the Receiver Port field, click the search help icon. A port selection popup appears showing available XML HTTP ports. Select SAP_CPI (CONNECTION TO RFC DESTINATION TO CPI) this is the port you will configure in WE21 that routes IDoc messages to the SAP to CPI RFC destination. (SAP_S4_CPI is an alternative entry shown for reference.)

Fig 9.3 – Receiver port selection popup showing SAP_CPI and SAP_S4_CPI XML HTTP ports

9.4 — Setting Output Mode and IDoc Type

After selecting the port, configure the remaining outbound options: set the Receiver Port to SAP_CPI, set the Output Mode to "Pass IDoc Immediately" (so the IDoc is sent in real time without collecting), and keep the subsystem setting as "Do not start subsystem." Under the IDoc Type section, click the search help for Basic Type.

Fig 9.4 – Outbound Options showing SAP_CPI port, Pass IDoc Immediately mode, and Basic Type field

9.5 — Selecting the IDoc Basic Type

The Basic type of selection popup appears, filtered to Message Type ORDERS and displaying 10 entries. Select ORDERS05 — this is the standard IDoc basic type for purchase order messages in S/4HANA. The other variants (ORDERS01 through ORDERS04) are older versions. Click the confirm checkmark.

Fig 9.5 – Basic type selection popup showing ORDERS01 through ORDERS05 — select ORDERS05

9.6 — Saving the Outbound Parameters

The completed Outbound Parameters screen now shows: Message Type = ORDERS, Receiver Port = SAP_CPI, Output Mode = Pass IDoc Immediately, IDoc Type Basic type = ORDERS05. The "Cancel Processing After Syntax Error" checkbox is selected by default. Click Save to finalize the partner profile configuration.

Fig 9.6 – Completed outbound parameters with ORDERS05 IDoc type and SAP_CPI port

Step 10: EDI Message Type and Output Type Assignment (WE81 / WE82)

Transactions WE81 and WE82 define the logical message types and their assignment to IDoc basic types. This mapping tells S/4HANA which IDoc structure corresponds to each EDI message type.

10.1 — EDI Logical Message Types (WE81)

Open Transaction WE81 (EDI: Logical Message Types Overview). This screen lists all (e.g.,2,355) defined message types. Click "New Entries" to add a custom message type entry for the ORDERS scenario. Note that standard entries like ORDERS already exist in the system, so this step may be for adding or verifying a custom mapping for the S4→IS scenario.

Fig 10.1 – WE81 EDI Logical Message Types overview with New Entries highlighted

10.2 — Adding the ORDERS Message Type

In the New Entries screen, create an entry with Message Type = ORDERS and Short Text = "SAP to SAP CPI." This creates a logical message type that links the business process (purchase order) with the IDoc interface. Click Save to commit the entry.

Fig 10.2 – New Entries for WE81 showing ORDERS message type with description "SAP to SAP CPI"

10.3 — Output Type Assignment to IDoc Type (WE82)

Open Transaction WE82 (Output Types and Assignment to IDoc Types Overview). This screen contains (e.g.,2,986) existing entries. Click "New Entries" to create the assignment linking the ORDERS message type to the ORDERS05 IDoc basic type.

Fig 10.3 – WE82 Output Types and Assignment to IDoc Types overview

10.4 — Saving the IDoc Type Assignment

In the New Entries screen, enter Message Type = ORDERS, Basic Type = ORDERS05, and Release = 100. This binding ensures that when an ORDERS message is dispatched, the system uses the ORDERS05 IDoc structure. Click Save.

Fig 10.4 – New Entry assigning ORDERS message type to ORDERS05 basic type with Release 100

Step 11: IDoc Reference Tools (WE30 and WE31)

Transactions WE30 and WE31 are used for developing and viewing IDoc type definitions. These are typically used in development to extend or inspect the IDoc structure. They are referenced here for completeness.

11.1 — Develop IDoc Types (WE30)

Transaction WE30 (Develop IDoc Types: Initial Screen) is used to create or modify IDoc basic types. The screen shows the Obj. Name field and a selection between Basic type and Extension. In a standard configuration scenario, you would use this to inspect the structure of ORDERS05 if needed.

Fig 11.1 – WE30 Develop IDoc Types initial screen

11.2 — Development Segments (WE31)

Transaction WE31 (Development Segments: Initial Screen) displays segment definitions for IDoc types. The Segment type field and the Definitions table (showing version, definition, release, and other metadata) are used when building or viewing custom segment structures for extended IDoc types.

Fig 11.2 – WE31 Development Segments initial screen

Step 12: Configuring the IDoc Port (Transaction WE21)

The IDoc Port defines the technical channel through which S/4HANA sends IDoc messages to external systems. For CPI communication, an XML HTTP port is used, which routes IDocs over HTTPS to the configured RFC Destination.

12.1 — Ports in IDoc Processing Overview

Open Transaction WE21 (Ports in IDoc Processing). In the left-hand Ports tree, you can see several categories: Transactional RFC, File, ABAP-PI, XML File, and XML HTTP. Expand XML HTTP — this is where CPI-facing ports are created. Click the Create icon to add a new XML HTTP port.

Fig 12.1 – WE21 Ports overview with XML HTTP category highlighted

12.2 — Configuring the SAP_CPI Port

After creating the port, the configuration screen appears. Fill in the following settings: Port name = SAP_CPI, Description = "CONNECTION TO RFC DESTINATION TO CPI," RFC Destination = "SAP to CPI" (the destination created in SM59). Under Content Type, select Text/XML. Set HTTP Version to Version 1.0 and check the SOAP Protocol checkbox — this ensures the IDoc payload is wrapped in a SOAP envelope when sent to CPI. Click Save.

Fig 12.2 – SAP_CPI port configuration with RFC destination, Text/XML content type, and SOAP Protocol enabled

Step 13: Testing IDoc Dispatch (Transaction WE19)

With all configurations in place, the IDoc Test Tool (WE19) is used to select an existing IDoc, update its control record to point to the CPI partner and port, and trigger Standard Outbound Processing to send it to CPI.

13.1 — Test Tool for IDoc Processing

Open Transaction WE19 (Test Tool for IDoc Processing). Select "Existing IDoc" as the template and click the search help icon to look up an available IDoc by number.

Fig 13.1 – WE19 Test Tool initial screen with Existing IDoc option selected

13.2 — Searching for an Existing IDoc

In the search dialog, enter the Created-On date range (e.g., 10.03.2026 to 12.03.2026) and click Execute. The result displays 7 IDoc entries with message type ORDERS, showing their IDoc numbers, statuses (02 or 03), ports (SAP_CPI or SAPA4H), partner types, and receiver partners (CPICLNT100). Select the most recent IDoc to use as a test template.

Fig 13.2 – IDoc search filter with date range set

13.3 — Selecting the IDoc Number

After choosing the IDoc from the search results, the test tool is populated with the selected IDoc number (e.g., 0000000000211067). Click the clock/refresh icon to load the full IDoc data.

Fig 13.3 – IDoc search results showing ORDERS IDocs routed to SAP_CPI and SAPA4H_CPI ports

13.4 — Editing the Control Record Fields

Click the "Standard Outbound Processing" button (Step 5 in the numbered flow). This opens the Edit Control Record Fields dialog. Configure the control record as follows: Receiver Port = SAP_CPI, Receiver Partner No. = CPICLNT100, Receiver Part. Type = LS. Sender Port = SAPA4H_CPI, Sender Partner No. = CPICLNT100, Sender Part. Type = LS. Set the Message Type = ORDERS. Click the green checkmark (Step 4) to confirm the control record changes, then click "Standard Outbound Processing" to trigger dispatch.

Fig 13.4 – Test tool with IDoc number 0000000000211067 loaded

13.5 — Triggering Outbound Processing

The Standard Outbound Processing of IDoc dialog confirms the dispatch parameters: "Start outbound IDoc processing now" is checked, Retry Factor = 1, Receiver Port = SAP_CPI, PortType = HTTP Port, and the Internet Address shows the CPI URL. Click the green checkmark to send the IDoc.

Fig 13.5 – Edit Control Record Fields dialog showing Receiver = SAP_CPI/CPICLNT100 and Sender = SAPA4H_CPI/CPICLNT100

Fig 13.6 – Outbound Processing dialog confirming SAP_CPI, HTTP port and CPI internet address

Step 14: Verifying the Message in CPI Monitor

After triggering the IDoc dispatch, switch to SAP Integration Suite to confirm the message was received and processed successfully by the IDOC_Inbound iFlow.

14.1 — SAP Integration Suite Monitor — Message Processing Overview

Navigate to Monitor → Integrations and APIs in SAP Integration Suite. The Monitor Message Processing section now shows 1 Message and 1 Completed Message in the "Past Hour" tiles — confirming that the IDoc was received and processed. Click on the "1 Completed" tile to view the message details.

Fig 13.1 – SAP Integration Suite Monitor showing 1 message received and 1 completed in the past hour

13.2 — Message Processing Details

The Monitor Message Processing screen shows two completed IDOC_Inbound message runs from Mar 12, 2026. Both show Status = Completed. Click on the first entry to view its properties. The Properties tab shows the Message ID, Correlation ID, and Sender = "Sender_IDOC." The Logs section shows Log Level = Trace (enabled for debugging) and Instance ID = 1.

Fig 13.2 – Monitor Message Processing showing two completed IDOC_Inbound message runs

13.3 — Integration Flow Model — Message Processing Run

Click on the first run to open the Message Processing Run details. The Run Steps panel (3 steps) shows the processing path: IDOC (Segment 1, < 3 ms) → End (Segment 1, 1 ms) → IDOC (Segment 1, 14 ms). The Integration Flow Model diagram on the right confirms the flow: Sender → IDOC adapter → Start → End → Receiver. All steps completed without error.

Fig 13.3 – Message Processing Run showing 3 steps and the integration flow diagram

13.4 — Message Content — Header Details

Click on the "End" step in the run view and switch to Message Content. The Header tab shows the message metadata including: SapIDocTransferId = 0000000000211075, SapIDocType = ORDERS05. This confirms that the IDoc number and type were correctly transmitted from S/4HANA to CPI and recognized by the integration flow.

Fig 13.4 – Message Content Header showing SapIDocTransferId and SapIDocType = ORDERS05

13.5 — Message Content — IDoc Payload

Click on the Payload tab to view the raw IDoc message received by CPI. The XML payload begins with <ORDERS05><IDOC BEGIN="1"><EDI_DC40 SEGMENT="1"> and contains all the IDoc control, data, and segment fields transmitted from S/4HANA. This includes the document number (DOCNUM=0000000000211075), routing info (SNDPRT=LS, RCVPRT=LS, SNDPRN=CPICLNT100, RCVPRN=CPICLNT100), and all E1EDK* business segments containing the order data. The Download Payload link allows saving the XML for further inspection.

Fig 13.5 – Message Payload tab showing the full ORDERS05 IDoc XML received by SAP Integration Suite

Step 15: Final IDoc Status Verification in S/4HANA (Transaction WE02)

The final step is to verify the IDoc status on the S/4HANA side using the IDoc List transaction (WE02), which confirms the outbound IDoc was dispatched successfully.

14.1 — IDoc List Search (WE02)

Open Transaction WE02 (IDoc List). On the Default tab, set the Created On date to (e.g.,12.03.2026) and enter the IDoc Number 0000000000211075. Click Execute to retrieve the IDoc record.

Fig 14.1 – WE02 IDoc List search screen with IDoc number and date filter

14.2 — IDoc Display: Status 03 — Data Passed to Port

The IDoc Display screen for IDoc 0000000000211075 confirms successful delivery. Under Short Technical Information: Direction = 1 (Outbound), Current Status = 03 (Data passed to port OK, shown with a green indicator). The IDoc structure tree on the left shows Control Record, Data Records, and Status Records (03, 30, 01, 42). The status record 03 — "Data passed to port OK" — is the key confirmation that the IDoc was successfully handed off to the SAP_CPI port and transmitted to CPI. Additional details: Message Type = ORDERS, Partner No. = CPICLNT100, Partn. Type = LS, Port = SAP_CPI.

Fig 14.2 – IDoc Display showing Status 03 (Data passed to port OK) and Port = SAP_CPI

Conclusion

This guide has walked through the complete end-to-end configuration required to send an IDoc from SAP S/4HANA to SAP BTP Integration Suite (IS). The following key milestones confirm successful integration:

  • RFC Destination "SAP to CPI" created in SM59 with HTTPS, Basic Authentication, and SSL Active
  • IDOC_Inbound iFlow deployed in SAP Integration Suite with endpoint /cxf/idoc_inbound
  • CPI SSL certificate imported into S/4HANA Trust Manager (STRUST) to establish SSL trust
  • Connection Test from SM59 returning HTTP 500 from CPI — confirming HTTPS connectivity
  • Partner Profile (WE20) configured for partner CPI (LS) with ORDERS / ORDERS05 / SAP_CPI outbound parameters
  • XML HTTP Port SAP_CPI (WE21) pointing to the "SAP to CPI" RFC destination with SOAP Protocol enabled
  • IDoc dispatched via WE19 Test Tool and received by IS with Status = Completed
  • IDoc Status in WE02 showing Status 03: Data passed to port OK

This integration pattern can be extended by adding message transformation, routing logic, or error handling steps within the Integration Suite iFlow to process the received IDoc data and forward it to downstream systems or services.

entry.title: Re: SmartOnboard VS SF Onboarding
entry.content: SuccessFactors Onboarding is the recommended solution. SmartRecruiters onboarding can be used as more of a "pre-boarding" solution, for customers who may need to collect data prior to initiating SF Onboarding. SR Pre-boarding is very light with limitations.

entry.title: Re: Business rules in SmartRecruiters
entry.content: Business Rules currently are not available in SmartRecruiters, however SmartRecruiters is built with automation. Automation is built in through the product with Auto-Advanced Rules, Org Fields and others. Additional Business Rule functionality is being looked at by the product management team. Please keep an eye out for the product releases coming around this.

entry.title: Re: Is there similar feature of Job profile Builder in Sm...
entry.content: Job Profile Builder integration is planned for the SR Phase 2 Integration timeline, later this year. Currently though, you can store job templates in SmartRecruiters today. These templates can store job description, qualifications and additional company information helpful for job postings. Additionally, SmartRecruiters has a Job Ad AI feature which assists with the creation of job descriptions.

entry.title: Re: Do you know what it takes to keep Fiori running smoothly?
entry.content:
Hi @saurabh_v 
Thank you for highlight this.
The link is now corrected.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Automated SAP Datasphere TaskChains with SAP CLI, Batch and Windows Scheduler
entry.content:
Introduction

In large SAP landscapes, Task Chains often run repeatedly and require monitoring. Manual supervision can therefore be time- and cost-consuming. By combining the SAP CLI, batch scripting, and Windows Task Scheduler, you can fully automate Task Chain execution, monitoring, and error handling.

Use Case: This automation approach is particularly suitable for Task Chains that may fail due to missing responses from source systems or because of long runtime/response times. In these cases, a simple automatic restart can resolve the issue. This method is not recommended for all Task Chains, as persistent errors may indicate a deeper problem that cannot be resolved by a restart.

Before continuing with the step-by-step guide on how to set up your own automated SAP Datasphere Task Chain, I will briefly describe the architecture of the automated SAP Datasphere Task Chain process.

Archtiecture Overview

The Windows Server hosts the Task Scheduler and the Batch Script. We need the the Task Scheduler to execute the Batch Script at configured intervals. The Batch Script contains logic to check the Task Chain status, writes logs and restart the TaskChain if needed. With the SAP Datasphere Command Line Interface (CLI) we have the possibility to acces our TaskChains via OAuth authentifaction. SAP Datasphere CLI acts as an interface between the Batch Script and SAP Datasphere Task Chain.

With this approach the TaskChains in SAP Datasphere are executed and monitored automatically.

Step-by-Step Guide

Install SAP Datasphere CLI

To control your Task Chain over the CLI the following prerequisites should be fulfilled:

  1. Install Node.js on Windows  ( https://nodejs.org/en/download/ )
  2. Install SAP Datapshere CLI globally with the following command

                            npm install -g @sap/datasphere-cli

      3. To check if the installation was sucessful, run:

                            datasphere --version

Make sure, that Node.js is correctly in your System Path , so the CLI Command is recognized.

Connect CLI to Datapshere Tenant

Configure CLI and log on to your SAP Datasphere Tenant  

datasphere config host set https://yourdataspheretenant.eu10.hcs.cloud.sap

datasphere login

 

To log into the Command Line Interface a OAuth2.0 Client with an interactive usage purpose is required. If you have an administrator role you can create OAuth2.0 Client by yourself. Follow the instructions in the SAP Help Portal "Create an OAuth2.0 Client with an Interactive Usage Purpose | SAP Help Portal"

After creating the client, log in with your Client ID and Secret. If the login is successful a confirmation window will appear. Afterwards you won't need to repeat the log in for 720 hours.

 

 

 

Test Task Chain Commands

Below are some commands you can test. Make sure they return the excpected results before proceeding with the automation of the Task Chain.

  1. Call the Stauts of a Task Chain:

              datasphere tasks logs get --space TRAINING_SPACE_1 --log-id 10183

       2. List all Logs of a Task Chain:

            datasphere tasks logs list --space TRAINING_SPACE_1 --objectname DEMO_TASK_CHAIN_CLI

      3. Execute a Task Chain manually:

            datasphere tasks chains run --space TRAINING_SPACE_1 --object DEMO_TASK_CHAIN_CLI

      4. You can list all available commands with the follwing command

            datasphere --h

Create a Batch File

After setting up the conection to SAP Datasphere through SAP CLI we will create a Batch Script not only to automate and monitor our task chains but also to restart task chains if the status is FAILED.

You can use the following script as a template:

@echooff

setlocal enabledelayedexpansion

set SPACE_ID=technical_name_of_your_space_ID

set TASKCHAIN_NAME=DEMO_TASK_CHAIN_CLI

set LOGFILE=C:\Users\...\Logs\taskchain_watch.log

 

if not exist "C:\Users\...\Logs" (

    mkdir "C:\Users\...\Logs"

)

 

REM determine last log ID

for /f "tokens=2 delims=:" %%a in ('

  datasphere tasks logs list --space %SPACE_ID% --objectname %TASKCHAIN_NAME% ^| findstr /i "logId"

') do (

  set LOG_ID=%%a

  set LOG_ID=!LOG_ID:,=!

  goto GET_STATUS

)

 

REM Check Status of Task Chain

:GET_STATUS

for /f %%a in ('

  datasphere tasks logs get --space %SPACE_ID% --log-id %LOG_ID%

') do (

  set STATUS=%%a

)

 

set STATUS=!STATUS:"=!

 

REM Neustart bei FAILED

if /i "!STATUS!"=="FAILED" (

    datasphere tasks chains run --space %SPACE_ID% --object %TASKCHAIN_NAME%

    echo %date% %time% FAILED erkannt – Task Chain neu gestartet >> "%LOGFILE%"

    goto :EOF

)

 

echo %date% %time% Status OK (!STATUS!) >> "%LOGFILE%"

 

Explanation:

  • @echooff  - suppresses display of commands
  • set -  definition of variables
  • for -  processing outputs in loops
  • if -  performs conditional checks
  • goto -  jumps to a defined script marker

Automate with Windows Task Scheduler

Now we move on to the automation part.

  1. Open Task Scheduler and click on "Create Task"
  2. On the General Tab give a name and Description  and also check "Only run, if usesr is logged" button

      3. On the Trigger Tab set up your individual Schedule (daily, weekly, monthly, etc.)

      4. On the Actions tab select your batch script 

After setting up your scheduled task make sure that logs are generated and the Task Chain restart by the Status FAILED.

 

 

Conclusion/Summary

By combining SAP CLI, Batch Script, and Windows Task Scheduler, you can now execute and monitor Task Chains automatically. Failures are detected and resolved by restarting the Task Chain, and logs are generated for traceability.

Optional enhancements:

  • Add email notifications for FAILED Task Chains
  • Monitor multiple Task Chains simultaneously
  • Centralize logs for team access
  • Limit the number of automatic restarts (e.g., max 3 retries) to identify persistent issues that require manual intervention

entry.title: A Use Case for HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph: AI‑Driven Tender Analysis
entry.content:
Introduction

Many companies face the same challenge: they need to match unstructured text, such as tenders, applications, or support tickets, with their internal data to make informed decisions. In our project, we tackled exactly this problem and worked on an application that connects text understanding, semantic search and structured data. Our primary objective was to acquire knowledge about the tools SAP offers for developing applications of this nature. We undertook this project as part of SAP’s Early Adopter Care program for the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine.

Our concrete use case was an internal tender analysis scenario within our company, focusing on public-sector calls for tenders. Deciding about potential participation requires answering several questions: Does the topic fit our company's portfolio, which skills and experience are required and do the required skills exist in our organization, are the necessary resources available? Instead of manually searching for this information through documents and databases, we wanted to automate this process. The goal was to build a system that can read and interpret the tender text, understand the company's internal skill profiles, and combine both to produce a meaningful recommendation.

To achieve this, we combined three capabilities:

  • semantic structuring of employee data using a knowledge graph
  • semantic representation of competency descriptions and CVs using vector embeddings
  • LLM-powered analysis to interpret and connect both worlds

This architecture allowed us to bring together unstructured text and structured data enabling precise matching and explainable results, thus building a foundation that can be reused for many other AI scenarios.

We implemented the application using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), a cloud-native framework that seamlessly integrates with SAP HANA Cloud, SAP AI Core and the Generative AI Hub (GenAI Hub). To give you a clear picture of how these components work together, the solution diagram below illustrates the overall flow, from data ingestion to semantic processing to the final application.

Solution Diagram

Provisioning the Resources

To implement this architecture, we provisioned a set of cloud resources in SAP BTP , each of which contribute a specific capability to the overall workflow.

At the center of the setup is SAP HANA Cloud, which provides both the Knowledge Graph Engine (KGE) and the Vector Engine (VE), and in which the CAP application data is persisted. The KGE is not enabled by default; it must be activated when the HANA instance is created and comes with some technical requirements:

  • Triple Store activation: The KGE relies on the HANA Triple Store, which must be enabled as part of the instance configuration. Without this, RDF triples cannot be stored or queried.
  • Minimum memory requirement: The KGE requires a HANA instance with at least 48 GB memory. This is necessary because storing the graph, executing SPARQL and SQL statements, and inference processes such as subclass inference, domain/range inference, and transitive property expansion operate in-memory. Smaller instances cannot activate Triple Store at all.

For the AI workflows, we provisioned AI Core which includes the GenAI Hub. AI Core runs the embedding and LLM processing jobs, while the GenAI Hub provides access to the models used for these tasks. This allows the application to generate semantic representations of text documents (tenders and CVs in our use case) and to retrieve information of documents via prompts.

The application layer itself is deployed as a CAP service running in a Cloud Foundry environment. CAP binds to the HANA Cloud, AI Core, and GenAI Hub service instances through standard service bindings, i.e. by receiving all required credentials and endpoints through environment variables.

This provisioning model keeps the architecture modular: each capability (knowledge graph, vector search, AI processing, and application logic) is provided as a separate cloud resource. It also makes the system easy to extend.

Knowledge Graphs and Embeddings: The Semantic Foundation of Our Architecture

Before diving into the SAP-specific components, it is useful to look at the two core concepts that shape our solution: knowledge graphs and embeddings. Both address the same challenge from different angles: turning human-readable meaning into structured, machine-interpretable representation.

Knowledge Graphs: Representing Relations between Data Entities

A knowledge graph stores information not as tables, but as entities and relationships. Instead of using foreign keys and joins, it forms an explicit network of connected facts. This makes it easier to express complex relationships, integrate data, and run queries that follow semantic structures rather than rigid schemas.

At the heart of every knowledge graph is an ontology: a structured definition of entities, properties, and relationships that the graph represents. An ontology defines:

  • classes (e.g., Person, Skill, Project)
  • properties that connect them (e.g., hasSkill, requiresSkill)
  • hierarchies and subclasses (e.g., abstract classes or hierarchical organization units)
  • constraints and expected value types (e.g., each identifiable entity has exactly one ID of type int)

The ontology gives the graph its semantic structure. It tells the system what kinds of things exist in the domain and how they relate to each other. Without an ontology, a knowledge graph would be just a collection of disconnected triples.

Knowledge graphs are typically stored as RDF (Resource Description Framework) triples, with the following general structure:

Subject – Predicate – Object
  • Subject: the thing we are talking about
  • Predicate: the property or relationship
  • Object: the value or another entity

For example:

Person123 – hasSkill – Python

This triple structure is simple but flexible and extensible. It is especially suitable for representing domains where the data model evolves over time.

To query RDF data, we use SPARQL, a graph-oriented query language. Instead of specifying joins explicitly – as is required in SQL – SPARQL expresses patterns that should appear in the data. This approach is particularly powerful for dynamic or AI-generated queries, where the structure of the query may not be known in advance. While SQL’s traditional query optimization excels when working with predefined schemas and foreign keys, it becomes less flexible when relationships or entity types vary. SPARQL, by contrast, is built to operate on semantic relationships and can naturally accommodate evolving graph structures.

The conceptual difference becomes clear when comparing a typical SQL query—which depends on rigid tables and join conditions—with an equivalent SPARQL query, where relationships are expressed in a way that is closer to how users understand the domain. In SPARQL, entities and relationships are simply assumed to exist in the graph without requiring predefined foreign-key constraints.

SQL-Query example

SELECT
    u.UserID,
    u.JobPosition,
    u.OrgUnitID,
    s.SkillName,
    us.SkillLevel,
    us.SkillLevelName,
FROM Users u
JOIN UserSkills us ON u.UserID = us.UserID
JOIN Skills s ON us.SkillID = s.SkillID
WHERE u.Active = 1

SPARQL-Query example

PREFIX : <http://example.org/my-project#>
PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
PREFIX xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#>

SELECT ?person ?position ?skill ?skillName ?level
WHERE {
    ?person rdf:type :Person ;
            :isActive true ;
            :hasJobPosition ?position ;
            :hasUserSkill ?userSkill .
    ?userSkill :connectsToSkill ?skill ;
               :hasSkillLevel ?level ;
               :hasSkillLevelName ?levelName .
    ?skill :hasSkillName ?skillName .   
}

 In short:

  • SQL is designed for handling stable, well-defined tabular schemas with fixed relationships.
  • SPARQL is designed for handling semantic relationships, flexible graph structures, and dynamically changing query shapes, making it well suited for AI-generated scenarios.

Embeddings: Encoding Text Semantics as Vectors

Embeddings take a different approach. Instead of modelling explicit relationships, they encode the semantic meaning of text as high-dimensional vectors. Similar texts end up close to each other in vector space while unrelated texts are far apart. This enables semantic search, similarity matching and clustering. Embeddings are essential when working with documents like tenders or CVs, where meaning is not explicitly structured but must be inferred.

How This Maps to the SAP World: KGE and VE

SAP HANA Cloud provides built-in components that implement these concepts directly inside the database:

  • The Knowledge Graph Engine (KGE) stores RDF triples and executes SPARQL queries. It gives us explicit, queryable relationships between entities such as people, skills, and projects.
  • The Vector Engine (VE) stores embeddings and provides functions for calculating the cosine similarity between embeddings, which simplifies similarity searches. It allows us to compare tender documents with CVs or skill profiles on a semantic level.

Together, KGE and VE combine explicit knowledge in the form of the knowledge graph structure with implicit meaning in the form of text embeddings. This dual representation enables our application to extract relevant information from tender documents and map it to our data model of employee competencies.

In the next section, we will look at how we designed the ontology and processed the data to populate both engines.

Building the Knowledge Graph

Creating the knowledge graph was a multi-step process that combined data engineering, domain modeling, and graph construction. While some parts of the pipeline can be automated, the core design work requires domain knowledge and deliberate modeling decisions. In our project we followed a three-stage approach.

Selecting and Structuring Relevant Data

The first step was to identify which data should become part of the knowledge graph and how these pieces of information relate to each other. This is a classic data engineering task: understanding the domain, exploring the available data sources, and deciding which entities and relationships are relevant for the scenario.

In our case, this meant analyzing the existing SQL-based data model (employees, skills, projects, roles, and assignments) and determining which parts of it contain meaningful information for matching tenders with internal capabilities. This step cannot be easily automated; it requires domain knowledge to decide which entities and relationships between them matter for the business question and which attributes are relevant for which entity type.

Creating An Ontology

Once the structure of the ontology has been decided on, it can be translated into classes, properties, hierarchies, and constraints that structure the graph.

Our ontology is defined using Turtle (Terse RDF Triple Language), a human-readable syntax for expressing RDF data. Turtle is a W3C standard that provides a compact and intuitive way to describe semantic relationships. It is based on the following key syntax elements:

Prefixes: Define namespace shortcuts for cleaner code

@prefix : <http://example.org/our-project#> .
@prefix owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .

Classes: Define entity types using a owl:Class

:Person a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "Person" ;
    rdfs:comment "Individual employee or consultant" .

Properties: Define relationships (ObjectProperty) or attributes (DatatypeProperty)

:possessesSkillWith a owl:ObjectProperty ;
    rdfs:domain :Person ;
    rdfs:range :UserSkill .

This links a person to their skill instances which include, e.g., proficiency levels and experience.

Triples: The fundamental RDF structure (Subject-Predicate-Object)

:Person rdfs:subClassOf :IdentifiableEntity .

This reads as: "Person is a subclass of IdentifiableEntity"

The semicolon notation allows defining multiple properties of the same entity without repeating the subject. The following statement is equivalent to four separate triples, but much more readable:

:Person a owl:Class ;
    rdfs:label "Person" ;
    rdfs:comment "Individual employee" ;
    rdfs:subClassOf :IdentifiableEntity .

To organize related concepts, we introduced abstract classes. They reduce redundancy by collecting shared properties in one place instead of repeating them across many similar classes. This makes the ontology easier to maintain and ensures consistency because updates to shared properties only need to be made once.

In addition to structuring the model, the ontology also defines formal constraints that describe how data in the knowledge graph is expected to look. These constraints specify rules and cardinalities, such as requiring each person to have exactly one UserID or ensuring that each project includes a start date. While SAP HANA Cloud cannot automatically enforce these constraints without a reasoner, they still serve as valuable documentation and help implementing validation checks using Shapes Constraint Language tools.

Creating Instances

The knowledge graph contains instances that are generated from data stored in a relational database. To connect both worlds, we defined a mapping that links SQL tables and columns to ontology properties. For example, the ontology property hasUserID corresponds to the column UserID in the table Users in an SQL database. This declarative mapping makes it possible to extract relational data and convert it into semantic triples .

Simple one‑to‑one and one‑to‑many relationships can be handled directly. For many‑to‑many relationships, we added an extended mapping layer that can follow junction tables in the relational schema.

Because SAP HANA Cloud’s RDF Store does not include a reasoner, inverse properties cannot be inferred automatically. They must be created explicitly during triple generation. To support this, our mapping configuration allows us to define inverse property pairs (e.g., tagsProject ↔ projectTaggedWith), so the parser generates both directions of the relationship automatically.

The triple generation pipeline runs in several steps. First, it creates datatype property triples (literal attributes), automatically assigning the correct types based on the ontology. Then it generates object property triples (links between entities), using an in-memory index to quickly look up target URIs. For large datasets, the system uses chunked batch processing to load triples in bulk, which improves performance and reduces memory usage.

Each ontology class is written into its own RDF graph, allowing updates to specific entity types without reloading the entire knowledge base. Finally, the system serializes these graphs into Turtle (.ttl) files and uploads them to the SAP Knowledge Graph Engine (i.e., SAP HANA Cloud’s RDF Store).

Requirement Analysis and Evaluation

Extracting required skills and experience levels via Prompts

To extract information from tender documents, we developed an LLM‑based pipeline that first checks whether a tender fits the company’s domain. For tenders that match the business profile, the pipeline automatically generates a concise text summary and extracts the skills referenced in the document. For each identified skill, it captures the skill name, and its stated importance level in the tender — classified as must-have, should-have, or nice-to-have (when explicitly indicated in the text).

Skills and priorities

Skill Matching

This structured extraction turns free‑text requirements into machine‑readable skill profiles that can be directly queried within our knowledge graph. For these queries, the system needs to match the skills extracted by the LLM with the skill names stored in the employee database. These names rarely match exactly because of synonyms or different wording. We have implemented this matching by first looking up the top-k skills most similar in terms of word embedding using the Knowledge Graph Engine and subsequently filtering out entries that are unrelated to the skill mentioned in the tender via a prompt (for example, matching “Python” in the tender to entries like “Python 3” or “Python Programming” in the employee database).

Once the skill mapping is complete, the matched skills are inserted into a predefined SPARQL query. This query returns all employees who have these skills, including their proficiency levels, enabling automated candidate identification for tender requirements.

By combining embeddings with LLMs, the system can handle linguistic variations while still generating precise SPARQL queries.

Users matching

 

Requirement Evaluation

Once employees with the required skills are found through the SPARQL queries, the system calculates a skill score based on their proficiency levels. A skill receives a score of 1.0 if at least one employee is an expert, 0.8 for intermediate proficiency, 0.5 for beginner-level proficiency, and 0.0 if no one in the company has that skill. This gives an overall picture of how well the organization covers each required competency.

The skill scores are aggregated within the importance levels (must‑have, should‑have, nice‑to‑have) using a simple average. The result is a score for each importance level that shows how well the company’s employees' competencies match the tender requirements at different levels of importance.

Aggregated scores

Supplementary Analysis: Comparing Employee CVs and Tender Text

Beyond the structured skill matching outlined above, we added a second analysis pipeline which determines the suitability of candidates for a given tender document by evaluating the semantic similarity between employee CVs and tender text based on embeddings.

Employee CVs are split into meaningful chunks (up to 6,000 tokens each) so that related information about projects and skills stays together. Each chunk is then embedded using a suitable model deployed through the SAP AI Core and stored in SAP Vector Engine (i.e. SAP HANA Cloud’s vector database) for semantic search. Tender documents go through the same embedding process, turning unstructured text into dense vectors that capture meaning beyond simple keywords.

To identify suitable candidates, the system compares CV embeddings with tender embeddings using cosine similarity, ranking employees according to how closely their experience aligns with the project requirements. By capturing semantic similarity rather than relying on exact keyword matches, this approach enables accurate matching even when different wording or terminology is used.

By combining precise skill matching through the knowledge graph with semantic search across CV embeddings, the system provides a comprehensive way to find candidates based both on explicitly listed competencies and experience descriptions.

Outlook

As a next step, we plan to enable fully automated tender analysis where LLMs generate SPARQL queries directly from the tender text, without relying on predefined templates. This means the LLM would translate project requirements into precise knowledge graph queries on its own. For example, if a tender asks for “three senior Java developers with cloud architecture experience and at least five years in the financial sector,” the LLM would automatically create a SPARQL query that filters employees by skill levels, domain experience, and project history, returning all candidates who meet these criteria in one step.

entry.title: Cloud Connector 2.19.0.2 is here!
entry.content:
One more time our development team delivered a fresh version with many updates and refinements. We recommend updating to 2.19.0.2 as soon as possible. If you’re already running a master–shadow setup, you can upgrade without downtime (follow the recommended high-availability upgrade path). The new build is available for download here.

What is new? As with previous updates, there are changes you’ll notice right away and others under the hood. Let’s take a quick tour—the complete list is in the release notes.

A few fixes to keep things smooth

  • System mappings via REST: You can now create entries with authenticationMode NONE_RESTRICTED using the API— andy when you don’t need principal propagation or a system-certificate logon.
  • Windows SNC helper scripts: The scripts for generating SNC configuration with the SAP Common Crypto Library now handle special characters in parameters and spaces in file names

 

Enhancements that make daily work easier

  • Broader platform coverage:
    • New OS and architecture support: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 16, and macOS 26 (Tahoe). Linux on aarch64 is now supported. Check the Product Availability Matrix
    • Runtime update: SapMachine 25 can be used as the Java runtime. Check the Prerequisites
  • Simpler trust management:
    • Optional automatic trust synchronization makes it easier to use the automatic signing key rotation of SAP Authorization and Trust Management. How to configure trusted entities in Cloud Connector. Learn more: Trusted Entities Configuration and Keyrotation
  • Governance and roles:
    • Managed subaccounts : These can be modified only by users with an administrator role; other users get read-only access for safer operations at scale. 
    • New role: AssociateAdministrator —a lighter-weight admin role to delegate tasks while keeping guardrails in place. 
  • Connectivity options:
    • WebSocket RFC as an alternative to classic CPIC‑based RFC when talking to ABAP systems —useful in modern network environments. How to configure: can be found here.
  • Operations and observability:
    • Monitoring REST API for alerts: New endpoints to query and integrate alert information with your monitoring stack.
    • More configuration REST APIs: Endpoints for alerting, e-mail, subject pattern rules, and principal propagation validity—making policy and configuration changes scriptable. See configuration APIs. 
    • Troubleshooting improvements: Additional categories with likely causes and suggested fixes to speed up analysis when something goes wrong.

Upgrade checklist

  • Verify OS and architecture support in the Product Availability Matrix
  • Confirm your Java runtime meets prerequisites
  • Plan a master–shadow upgrade to avoid downtime and follow the recommended procedure.
  • Consider enabling automatic trust synchronization to simplify signing key rotation.
  • Review roles and governance: use AssociateAdministrator where suitable; adopt managed subaccounts.
  • Evaluate switching ABAP RFC traffic to WebSocket RFC if it better fits your landscape.

We’re excited about this release—broader platform choice, simpler trust, stronger governance, and improved visibility all help keep hybrid connectivity reliable and secure. Happy updating!

entry.title: Link Handlers cannot be registered as the application is signed - Eclipse MAC
entry.content:
What Are Link Handlers?

When you click an adt:// link somewhere — SAP GUI, a wiki, a colleague's message — macOS needs to figure out which app should open it. That mapping is handled by link handlers.

In Eclipse, you find them under Preferences → General → Link Handlers. Two entries matter for ABAP developers:

  • adt – How adt:// links open ABAP objects (classes, programs, etc.) in your Eclipse workspace.
  • eclipse+command – Lets URLs trigger Eclipse commands, like opening a specific view.

Both should show a checked box and "This application" in the Handler column. If you only have one Eclipse on your Mac, you might never notice a problem. But with two installations (say one for ABAP, one for Java), wrong link routing gets annoying fast.

The Problem

You open Link Handlers and see this at the top:

⛔Link Handlers cannot be registered as the application is signed.

Checkboxes grayed out. Handler column says "Other application." Sometimes you also get this when clicking around the page:

Cannot invoke "org.eclipse.urischeme.ISchemeInformation.schemeIsHandledByOther()"
because "schemeInfo.information" is null

You click an adt:// link and either the wrong Eclipse opens, or nothing happens.

Why This Happens on macOS

macOS maps URL schemes to apps using the Info.plist inside each app bundle (/Applications/Eclipse.app/Contents/Info.plist). A key called CFBundleURLTypes lists which schemes the app claims. macOS reads it and stores the mapping in its Launch Services database. On top of that, plugins like ADT can also register schemes at runtime.

Here's where it breaks. Eclipse ships code-signed. The signature covers the entire bundle, including Info.plist. When Eclipse tries to modify the plist at runtime to register a scheme, macOS blocks it because that would invalidate the signature. The Link Handlers page sees the restriction and grays everything out.

The Fix

Two parts to this: first, make sure your Info.plist has the right URL scheme entries, then fix the code signature that's blocking everything.

⚠️Close Eclipse before you start.

Step 1: Check your Info.plist for CFBundleURLTypes

Open the plist:

sudo nano /Applications/Eclipse.app/Contents/Info.plist

Look for a CFBundleURLTypes key. The default Eclipse plist usually already has eclipse+command and eclipse+mpc, but not adt. You need all three in a single CFBundleURLTypes array. If the key already exists, add the adt entry into the existing array. If it's missing entirely, add this block just before the closing </dict> at the end of the file:

<key>CFBundleURLTypes</key>
<array>
    <dict>
        <key>CFBundleURLName</key>
        <string>Eclipse Command</string>
        <key>CFBundleURLSchemes</key>
        <array>
            <string>eclipse+command</string>
        </array>
    </dict>
    <dict>
        <key>CFBundleURLName</key>
        <string>Eclipse Marketplace</string>
        <key>CFBundleURLSchemes</key>
        <array>
            <string>eclipse+mpc</string>
        </array>
    </dict>
    <dict>
        <key>CFBundleURLName</key>
        <string>ADT Link Handler</string>
        <key>CFBundleURLSchemes</key>
        <array>
            <string>adt</string>
        </array>
    </dict>
</array>

Save and exit (Ctrl+O, then Ctrl+X).

Important: Make sure there's only one CFBundleURLTypes key in the entire file. If you're adding the adt entry to an existing array, don't create a second CFBundleURLTypes block — duplicate keys in a plist don't merge. macOS only reads one of them, and you'll get unpredictable results. I made this mistake editing in nano and it caused the schemeInfo.information is null error.

Step 2: Remove the existing code signature

sudo codesign --remove-signature /Applications/Eclipse.app

Don't try to open Eclipse after this. macOS won't launch an unsigned app. Move straight to Step 3.

Step 3: Re-sign with an ad-hoc signature

sudo codesign --force --deep --sign - /Applications/Eclipse.app

The - means ad-hoc — no developer certificate needed. --deep signs all the nested binaries inside the bundle too.

Step 4: Re-register with Launch Services

/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreServices.framework/Versions/A/Frameworks/LaunchServices.framework/Versions/A/Support/lsregister -R -f /Applications/Eclipse.app

Tells macOS to re-read the app bundle and update its URL scheme mappings.

Step 5: Open Eclipse and check

Go to Preferences → General → Link Handlers. Both adt and eclipse+command should now show checked and marked "This application".

All terminal commands together

# Close Eclipse first, then:
sudo codesign --remove-signature /Applications/Eclipse.app
sudo codesign --force --deep --sign - /Applications/Eclipse.app
/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreServices.framework/Versions/A/Frameworks/LaunchServices.framework/Versions/A/Support/lsregister -R -f /Applications/Eclipse.app
# Open Eclipse → Preferences → Link Handlers

What I Tried That Didn't Work

Sharing these so you don't waste time on the same dead ends.

Re-signing without removing first. Tried just running codesign --force --deep --sign - on its own. Didn't help. The old signature's restrictions stuck around somehow. You have to remove first, then sign fresh.

Duplicate CFBundleURLTypes keys in the plist. This one cost me the most time. When editing in nano, I accidentally added a second CFBundleURLTypes block instead of adding entries into the existing one. Plist files don't merge duplicate keys — macOS only reads one of them. The result was the schemeInfo.information is null error, and eclipse+command stayed stuck on "Other application." If you see that error, open the plist and check for duplicate keys.

Running lsregister by itself. Doesn't do anything useful if the signature is still blocking registration. Necessary as a final step, but not enough on its own.

Environment

Tested on Eclipse 2025-03 (4.39) with ADT on macOS. Should work the same on any recent Eclipse version where you see the signed-application warning.

entry.title: Re: Error when taking AI-based exam - waiting for reply f...
entry.content:
Together with support we figured out a likely candidate for the error: I had a Yoodli account with the same email address, and this seems to have tripped up the Single Sign-On process.
In my case the solution was to delete the Yoodli account (log in to your Yoodli account and there is a setting to delete the account somewhere). 

Thanks to @Margit_Wagner and @ashwani_gupta64 for the assistance!

Going forward it should be noted somewhere not to create a Yoodli account. The reason I did was to familiarise myself with the Yoodli platform as described in this blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-members/how-to-take-sap-s-new-performance-based-certification-exams/ba-p/14299781

There is one more thing: after I completed the exam, the analysis page said 13/15 points (87%), but the exam was failed. I'm confident we will figure this one out as well.
